case_name: Perchloroethylene-contaminated reservoir water for groundwater recharge under strict effluent, space, and moderate cost constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for Tetrachloroethylene-contaminated reservoir water intended for groundwater recharge while balancing strict effluent compliance, limited footprint, and moderate cost constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A utility plans to seasonally divert reservoir water to an engineered recharge basin, but source monitoring has identified low-µg/L contamination by 1,1,2,2-tetrachloroethylene,Ethylene tetrachloride,PCE,PERC,Perchloroethene,Perchloroethylene,Tetrachloroethene associated with an upstream urban runoff and legacy dry-cleaning drainage corridor. The recharge permit sets a very low finished-water target for volatile chlorinated solvents, and the treatment system must fit inside an existing compact pump station site. Several treatment routes are technically capable, including air stripping, adsorption, and membrane-based options, but ranking depends on how they trade off footprint, operator burden, off-gas or residuals handling, and total life-cycle cost. Because the reservoir water quality changes seasonally, the preferred option should also remain reliable when turbidity and natural organic matter increase during algal periods.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should reliably achieve a very low Tetrachloroethylene concentration suitable for groundwater recharge permit compliance, with stable performance during seasonal source-water variability.
secondary_goals: minimize treatment footprint at the existing site
avoid excessive off-gas or difficult residuals handling
maintain moderate capital and operating cost
reduce sensitivity to turbidity and organic-matter fluctuations

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer manageable residual streams; avoid solutions that create high-burden concentrate disposal or complicated air-emission control unless justified by compliance benefit
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: system should maintain solvent removal despite seasonal increases in turbidity and natural organic matter in reservoir water

core_unit_functions.compact_clarification_or_inline_coagulat: reduce turbidity and part of the natural organic matter to stabilize downstream operation
core_unit_functions.pressure_filtration: remove solids carryover and protect stripping equipment from fouling
core_unit_functions.packed_tower_air_stripping_with_vapor_ph: transfer volatile Tetrachloroethylene from water to air and capture stripped vapors to control emissions

applicability_rationale: Tetrachloroethylene is a volatile chlorinated solvent and air stripping is a well-established high-removal technology for VOC-contaminated water
the strict effluent target favors a technology with strong mass-transfer removal rather than sole reliance on liquid-phase adsorption
a packed-tower system can be designed with a relatively compact footprint compared with larger contactor basins or multiple large GAC vessels
off-gas polishing with vapor-phase GAC addresses air-emission concerns and makes the route more acceptable for a constrained urban site
pretreatment improves reliability under reservoir-water turbidity swings and reduces operational upset risk
potential_risks: air stripping performance depends on hydraulic and mass-transfer design and can be affected by fouling if pretreatment is inadequate
off-gas control media replacement and air permitting add residuals and compliance management requirements
cold-weather or lower-temperature operation may require design margin to maintain very low finished concentrations

core_unit_functions.compact_clarification_or_inline_coagulat: lower suspended solids and partially reduce organic fouling load
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: protect adsorption beds by removing remaining particulates
core_unit_functions.liquid_phase_GAC_dual_bed: adsorb Tetrachloroethylene and provide staged breakthrough protection

applicability_rationale: GAC is a plausible and commonly used route for chlorinated solvent polishing at low µg/L concentrations
dual-bed operation improves compliance reliability under strict finished-water requirements by reducing breakthrough risk
this route avoids off-gas handling and may be simpler to permit than air stripping in some locations
it is attractive where moderate cost and straightforward treatment train integration are important
potential_risks: higher DOC and seasonal NOM in reservoir water can consume adsorption capacity and shorten run length
multiple large vessels may challenge the available footprint compared with a vertical stripper configuration
spent carbon replacement or reactivation logistics must be planned carefully to avoid breakthrough

core_unit_functions.compact_clarification_or_inline_coagulat: reduce particulate and organic loading before downstream treatment
core_unit_functions.pressure_filtration: provide solids removal and protect polishing adsorber performance
core_unit_functions.low_profile_diffused_aeration_or_tray_ae: remove a portion of volatile Tetrachloroethylene with simpler aeration hardware
core_unit_functions.liquid_phase_GAC_polishing: capture residual dissolved solvent to reach the stringent finished-water target

applicability_rationale: the hybrid route spreads removal across volatilization and adsorption, reducing full dependence on either one process
it can be useful when a full packed tower is difficult to site or when operators want additional polishing assurance
GAC polishing helps compensate for the lower stripping efficiency of compact aeration approaches
this option may fit moderate cost constraints better than membrane systems while still supporting low effluent levels
potential_risks: open or shallow aeration approaches are generally less efficient than packed towers and may be sensitive to temperature and air-water contact limitations
combined process train increases coordination needs for operators
if influent spikes occur, GAC polishing demand may rise sharply

core_unit_functions.microfiltration_or_ultrafiltration_pretr: remove particulates and protect membrane integrity
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide broad-spectrum dissolved contaminant reduction including Tetrachloroethylene
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust treated water chemistry before recharge

applicability_rationale: RO is technically capable and offers broad contaminant control beyond the target solvent
the compact skid layout can be attractive where footprint is extremely limited
this route may be considered if future water-quality objectives expand beyond VOC removal
potential_risks: concentrate management is a major disadvantage for groundwater recharge projects and may be difficult to permit or dispose
higher energy use and membrane O&M burden conflict with the moderate cost objective
pretreatment and scaling control add complexity not strictly necessary for a volatile-solvent-dominant case

core_unit_functions.compact_clarification: reduce solids and improve UV transmittance relative to raw reservoir water
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process_uv_hydrogen_p: apply oxidative treatment intended for degradable organic micropollutants

applicability_rationale: included as a technically conceivable but poorly aligned option under these constraints
AOP systems can address some organic micropollutants but are not typically the preferred primary treatment for PCE-dominant water compared with volatilization or adsorption
potential_risks: Tetrachloroethylene is not generally the most favorable target for standalone AOP compared with established VOC removal technologies
strict effluent compliance may be harder to guarantee without an additional proven polishing barrier
UV system performance is sensitive to water quality and requires chemical handling and process monitoring

preferred_rank_pattern: air_stripping_based_routes should rank near the top for volatile Tetrachloroethylene under strict effluent limits
GAC should appear as a strong alternative but not automatically first because reservoir DOC and footprint affect performance and sizing
membrane options should not rank first due to concentrate handling and cost misalignment despite technical capability

must_reflect: strict finished-water target for groundwater recharge compliance
high footprint limitation at an existing compact site
moderate capital and operating cost sensitivity
trade-off between off-gas handling for stripping and media consumption for adsorption

must_contain: why Tetrachloroethylene volatility makes air stripping highly relevant
why reservoir-water DOC and turbidity can penalize GAC-only strategies and require pretreatment
why RO is downgraded mainly because of concentrate disposal, energy, and complexity rather than lack of treatment capability

should_mention: off-gas emissions control needs for stripping systems
adsorbent breakthrough and spent carbon management
seasonal source-water variability affecting treatment robustness
concentrate disposal burden for membrane systems
